lessons.title: What Is a Branch? The Lightweight Pointer Model
lessons.learning_objective: By the end of this lesson, learners will be able to explain what a branch is and understand that branches are lightweight pointers to commits, not copies of files.
lessons.notes: Emphasize: branch is NOT a copy of files, just a 41-byte pointer. Show ASCII diagram with multiple branches pointing to different commits.

lessons.title: Creating Branches with `git branch` and `git switch -c`
lessons.learning_objective: By the end of this lesson, learners will be able to create new branches using `git branch` and `git switch -c` (or `git checkout -b`).
lessons.notes: Two approaches: (1) create then switch separately, (2) create and switch in one command. Show legacy `git checkout -b` alongside modern `git switch -c`.

lessons.title: Listing and Viewing Branches
lessons.learning_objective: By the end of this lesson, learners will be able to list local and remote branches and identify the currently checked-out branch.
lessons.notes: Show `git branch` (local only), then `git branch -a` (all including remote-tracking). The asterisk marks current branch.

lessons.title: Switching Between Branches with `git switch`
lessons.learning_objective: By the end of this lesson, learners will be able to switch between branches and understand that the working directory updates to match the checked-out commit.
lessons.notes: Key safety rule: switching branches updates your working directory. You must commit or stash uncommitted changes first or Git refuses.

lessons.title: Understanding Divergent Histories
lessons.learning_objective: By the end of this lesson, learners will be able to recognize when two branches have diverged and understand the implications for merging.
lessons.notes: Show: (1) linear history (no divergence, fast-forward possible), (2) diverged history (both branches have new commits, three-way merge required).

lessons.title: Fast-Forward Merges
lessons.learning_objective: By the end of this lesson, learners will be able to perform a fast-forward merge and understand when Git uses this strategy.
lessons.notes: Show: switch to main, `git merge feature`. When feature is ahead of main with no divergence, Git simply moves main pointer forward.

lessons.title: Three-Way Merges and Merge Commits
lessons.learning_objective: By the end of this lesson, learners will be able to perform a three-way merge and understand how Git creates a merge commit when histories diverge.
lessons.notes: When both branches have new commits after a common ancestor, Git creates a merge commit with two parents. This records the integration event.

lessons.title: Deleting Branches
lessons.learning_objective: By the end of this lesson, learners will be able to delete merged branches with `git branch -d` and understand the safety implications.
lessons.notes: `-d` is safe (refuses to delete unmerged branches). `-D` forces deletion. Explain that deleting a branch pointer doesn't delete commits (still in reflog).

lessons.title: Feature Branch Workflow
lessons.learning_objective: By the end of this lesson, learners will be able to apply the feature branch workflow to isolate feature development and integrate changesets cleanly.
lessons.notes: Outline: (1) create branch from main, (2) work and commit, (3) push to remote, (4) open PR, (5) merge after review. This is industry-standard.
